title: Lessons from the School of Prayer: Praying for Others
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: Lessons from the School of Prayer
source_files: Lessons from the School of Prayer 4 - Praying for Others.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Two Dangers to Avoid
subpoints: Christians are not mere humanitarians — serving people is not the same as serving God
Physical health became the ultimate good during the pandemic — a dangerous distortion
But neither can we claim high intimacy with God while fostering no intimacy with people
Love for God and love for brethren are not identical but each tests the presence of the other

outline.title: Paul's 40 Prayers: Categories and Pattern
subpoints: Paul has approximately 40 passages dealing with prayer across his writings
These fall into four categories: prayers written in real time, prayer reports, prayer wishes, and prayer exhortations
Paul's prayers begin with thanksgiving for the Romans' faith spoken of throughout the world
He prays for his own people Israel to be saved — praying with intense personal passion

outline.title: The Character of Paul's Intercession
subpoints: Paul's mind is never idle — he strives for unceasing, steadfast prayer
He prays for church unity — not just peace, but unity for the glory of God
He prays for joy, peace, and hope by the power of the Holy Spirit
He asks the Romans to strive together with him in prayer — active, agonizing prayer

outline.title: Two Lessons in Praying for Others
subpoints: Lesson 1: Always submit to God's definition of what is good — pray the Bible back to God
Lesson 2: Praying for others demands examining our own hearts first
Unconfessed sin separates us from God and blocks our prayers (Isaiah 59:1-2)
Bitterness, grudges, and an unforgiving heart are the greatest obstacles to effective intercession

outline.title: Call to a Praying Church
subpoints: We need to know people well enough to pray specifically and knowingly for them
Even without knowing people we can pray for them — all humans share common temptations and needs
The church needs not more prayer times but to use existing prayer times more seriously
Praying for revival requires wanting revived people — specific people with names and faces and annoying habits

summary:
In the fourth lesson of the school of prayer series, Pastor Tito Lyro turns to the subject of praying for others, grounding it in a survey of Paul's 40 prayer passages across his epistles. Before diving into those texts, the lesson first addresses two ditches that Christians can fall into: the ditch of mere humanitarianism (reducing Christian life to social service), and the ditch of claiming intimacy with God while fostering none with people. Both love for God and love for the brethren are non-negotiable, though they are not the same — the presence of one tests the reality of the other.

The lesson surveys several of Paul's prayers in Romans, showing Paul's pattern: he thanks God for others' faith, prays passionately for Israel's salvation, strives in unceasing prayer, prays for church unity oriented toward God's glory, and asks the Romans to strive together with him in prayer as a corporate discipline. The focus of Paul's intercessions is consistently the spiritual good of others — their growth, their unity in Christ, their hope by the Holy Spirit's power.

Two lessons for praying for others emerge. First, always submit to God's definition of what is good — pray the Bible back to God. The Puritans said that God loves to hear His own words, so saturate yourself in Scripture and let it flow into prayer. Second, praying for others demands examining your own heart. Unconfessed sin, nurtured sin, and above all an unforgiving heart will block your prayers (Isaiah 59:1-2; Mark 11:25-26; 1 Peter 3:7). Bitterness and grudges toward brothers in Christ make prayer little more than wasted time and effort. The call to the church is a call to genuine, corporate, heart-examined intercession.
transcript:
## Introduction: Praying for Others

Tonight we're considering the subject of praying for others — the fourth lesson in our series on prayer. This whole series draws on Paul's theology of prayer, and tonight we will look at quite a few of his prayers as we build the case for why and how we ought to pray for one another.

Prayer is a lifeline to God who answers according to His blessed will and wisdom. He promises to answer all the prayers of His people who come to Him in the name of Jesus.

> *"Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need."* (Hebrews 4:16)

I believe we need a revival of prayer in our church — individually, publicly, and corporately as we gather together.

## Two Ditches to Avoid

Before we look at Paul's prayers, we need to identify two dangers — two ditches — that we can easily fall into as Christians.

**Ditch 1: Mere humanitarianism.** We must never think that serving people is the ultimate good, or suppose that serving people is exactly the same as serving God. They are not necessarily the same thing. In Jesus' teaching, the first command is love God with all your heart, mind, soul, and strength — and then the second is love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:37–40). Jesus numbers them in terms of priority — the first and the second. We are not just humanitarians. We are not merely about making this life better for people on earth. I think the church has fallen into this pit during difficult seasons — when physical health becomes the ultimate good and preserving physical life becomes the most important thing the church seeks to do. Scripture tells us not to fear the one who can destroy the body but to fear the one who can destroy both body and soul in hell (Matthew 10:28). There are greater consequences to our soul than just to our body.

**Ditch 2: Claiming intimacy with God while fostering no intimacy with people.** We cannot testify of deep love for Christ while nurturing jealousy and bitterness against the people around us. Those two things do not go together.

> *"If someone says, 'I love God,' and hates his brother, he is a liar; for he who does not love his brother whom he has seen, how can he love God whom he has not seen?"* (1 John 4:20)

So love for God and love for brothers must not be equated — they are not the same thing — but one will be tested by the other. The presence of one requires the presence of the other. When we live up to our calling to love, we remember that in God's church, people do not set the agenda — people *are* the agenda.

## Paul's Four Categories of Prayer

There are roughly forty passages in Paul's writings that include prayer, and they fall into four broad categories: prayers he appears to be praying as he writes; prayer reports where he tells his readers what he has prayed for them; prayer wishes or requests; and exhortations to prayer. Let's look at a sampling of these in Romans.

**Romans 1:8–10:**

> *"First, I thank my God through Jesus Christ for you all, that your faith is spoken of throughout the whole world... that without ceasing I make mention of you always in my prayers, making request if, by some means, now at last I may find a way in the will of God to come to you."*

Two things Paul is doing: giving thanks for the church, and praying that he may be with them to encourage them.

**Romans 10:1:**

> *"Brethren, my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they may be saved."*

He prays for those closest to him, his own ethnic family, with such intensity that he says elsewhere he could even wish himself accursed for their sake if it were possible for them to be saved.

**Romans 12:12:**

> *"...rejoicing in hope, patient in tribulation, continuing steadfastly in prayer..."*

Prayer in Paul's mind is continuous and steadfast — not a moment of idle thought in his heart. His mind is always engaged, and often engaged in prayer for God's people.

**Romans 15:5–6:**

> *"Now may the God of patience and comfort grant you to be like-minded toward one another, according to Christ Jesus, that you may with one mind and one mouth glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ."*

Unity in the church — not just for the sake of peace, but for the glory of God.

**Romans 15:30–33:**

> *"Now I beg you, brethren, through the Lord Jesus Christ, and through the love of the Spirit, that you strive together with me in prayers to God for me, that I may be delivered from those in Judea who do not believe, and that my service for Jerusalem may be acceptable to the saints..."*

*Strive together with me in prayer.* That is not a passive word. It is active, moving, struggling — an agonizing in prayer together for common goals.

## Two Lessons in Praying for Others

### 1. Submit to God's Definition of What Is Good

As we pray for others, we must always submit to God's definition of what is good. We pray for what is good for another as *God* defines it. That is why it is so important to listen to the prayers of the Bible — to let the Bible dictate what we pray. The Puritans used to say that God loves to hear His own words. So pray the Bible back to God for one another.

Ideally, you will read your Bible and then let that reading flow into prayer for others. The Bible should vibrate into prayer for the people around you.

### 2. Examine Your Own Heart

If you are going to pray effectively for others, you must examine your own heart. Unconfessed sin will cut off communication with God.

> *"Behold, the Lord's hand is not shortened, that it cannot save; nor His ear heavy, that it cannot hear. But your iniquities have separated you from your God; and your sins have hidden His face from you, so that He will not hear."* (Isaiah 59:1–2)

Our sins can get in the way of our praying. One of the most common obstacles to effective intercession is bitterness, resentment, grudges, and an unforgiving heart.

> *"Whenever you stand praying, if you have anything against anyone, forgive him, that your Father in heaven may also forgive you your trespasses. But if you do not forgive, neither will your Father in heaven forgive your trespasses."* (Mark 11:25–26)

Your prayer is not heard if you are holding grudges and bitterness in your heart. Peter even addresses this directly to husbands:

> *"Husbands, likewise, dwell with them with understanding, giving honor to the wife, as to the weaker vessel, and as being heirs together of the grace of life, that your prayers may not be hindered."* (1 Peter 3:7)

How can we intercede for our church if we secretly hold some of its members in contempt? How can we pray for revival if what we want is some abstract notion of reviving instead of *specific, named people* being made alive again by the Spirit of God?

It is high time for us to examine our hearts honestly and humbly. If we harbor bitterness and resentment, our praying is little more than wasted time and effort. But if our hearts are free of bitterness toward each other, then the Lord is going to bless our church. May He grant us the grace to repent, to seek forgiveness, and to pray with clean hands.
